coredata.dc:title: Identifying three routes of the sensing mechanism for casein-directed gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shanshan
Wang, Yingyi
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Although there have existed kinds of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) employed for various sensing purposes, scarce studies were proposed for systematically exploring their multiple routes of the sensing mechanism since that plays the critical role for broadening their analysis applications. Herein, casein-directed gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@casein) have been successfully raised for identifying their multiple routes towards the sensing mechanism. Initially, the current nanoclusters were synthesized in aqueous solution with a quantum yield of nearly 2.1%, which obviously showed the red fluorescence at 600 nm. Subsequently, we have explored three routes of quenching the fluorescence of AuNCs@casein by introducing thiourea, bromelain, and graphene oxide with amino groups (GO-NH2) as the representatives. To be specific, the formed bonds of Au-S between thiourea and AuNCs@casein achieved to quench their fluorescence, and the templates of AuNCs@casein destroyed by bromelain also showed the dramatically decreased fluorescence, and the energy transferring from the current AuNCs to GO-NH2 indeed lead to the fluorescent variations of AuNCs@casein. On the basis of these phenomena, AuNCs@casein was further applied for simply sensing thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2 through the standard formats. Significantly, this study elaborated three quenching routes of AuNCs@casein and may provide more sensing strategies for other types of nanoclusters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Casein-directed
Three routes
Sensing mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction between AuNCs and thiourea, bromelain, GO-NH2
Optimizing the synthesis conditions of AuNCs@casein
Quenching of AuNCs@casein through three routes
Establishing the sensing system for thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesizing AuNCs@casein and their three sensing routes.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs@casein, Inset: photographs of AuNCs@casein with daylight (a) and UV light (b); (B) UV-vis absorption spectrum of AuNCs@casein; (C) HR-TEM image of AuNCs@casein; (D) FTIR spectrum for this AuNCs; (E) XPS of AuNCs@casein; (F) XPS for Au 4f of AuNCs@casein.

(A) Illustration for quenching AuNCs@casein by thiourea; (B) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs in the absence (red) and presence (black) of 1 mM thiourea; Inset: photographs of AuNCs@casein in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 1 mM thiourea under UV light; (C) Response of AuNCs@casein to thiourea (1 mM) and other related substances including thioacetamide, Na2S, vitamin B5, procaine, nicotinic acid, Ca2+ and Ba2+ (10 mM for each); (D) Au 4f XPS spectra of AuNCs@casein without or with thiourea. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Scheme of identifying the interaction between bromelain and AuNCs@casein; (B) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs@casein without and with 500 U/mL bromelain, Inset: images of AuNCs without (a) and with (b) of 500 U/mL bromelain under UV light; (C) Response of AuNCs@casein to the proteins including bromelain, trypsin, papain, pepsin and BSA; (D) DLS analyses of AuNCs@casein in the absence and presence of bromelain.
(A) Scheme for establishing FRET system by GO-NH2 and AuNCs@casein; (B) Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding images of AuNCs@casein (a) and AuNCs@casein by introducing GO-NH2 (b).

Fluorescence spectra of the current sensing model with varied concentrations of thiourea (A), bromelain (D) and GO-NH2 (G); The linear relationships between F0-F and different concentrations of thiourea (B), bromelain (E) and GO-NH2 (H) respectively; Images of AuNCs@casein with different concentrations of thiourea (C), bromelain (F) and GO-NH2 (I) under daylight (a) and UV light (b).

head.ce:title: Identifying three routes of the sensing mechanism for casein-directed gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Silkworm Genome Biology, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silkworm Genome Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although there have existed kinds of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) employed for various sensing purposes, scarce studies were proposed for systematically exploring their multiple routes of the sensing mechanism since that plays the critical role for broadening their analysis applications. Herein, casein-directed gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@casein) have been successfully raised for identifying their multiple routes towards the sensing mechanism. Initially, the current nanoclusters were synthesized in aqueous solution with a quantum yield of nearly 2.1%, which obviously showed the red fluorescence at 600 nm. Subsequently, we have explored three routes of quenching the fluorescence of AuNCs@casein by introducing thiourea, bromelain, and graphene oxide with amino groups (GO-NH2) as the representatives. To be specific, the formed bonds of Au-S between thiourea and AuNCs@casein achieved to quench their fluorescence, and the templates of AuNCs@casein destroyed by bromelain also showed the dramatically decreased fluorescence, and the energy transferring from the current AuNCs to GO-NH2 indeed lead to the fluorescent variations of AuNCs@casein. On the basis of these phenomena, AuNCs@casein was further applied for simply sensing thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2 through the standard formats. Significantly, this study elaborated three quenching routes of AuNCs@casein and may provide more sensing strategies for other types of nanoclusters.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are one critical member of nanoclusters, and appear as the ultra-small particles with the obvious fluorescence and have attracted the special attention of researchers over the past ten years [1–5]. AuNCs, usually consisting of several to a hundreds of atoms, exist with the sizes of nearby Fermi wavelength [6,7], and show their unique properties including the satisfactory photostability, large stokes shift, long lifetime and acceptable biocompatibility, which are regulated by their subnanometer dimensions [8–11]. Hence, various methods have been developed for synthesizing fluorescent AuNCs. Basically, there have emerged two major routes for elucidating the recent techniques to prepare AuNCs. The first way is gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with the larger sizes are etched by the thiol compounds such as mercaptopropionic acid, facilitating to the formation of gold nanoclusters with a smaller size [12]. Another one is achieved by the reduction of Au3+ in the presence of the templates such as the small molecules or proteins [13,14]. In general, the second route serves as the popular way for synthesizing AuNCs. To be specific, the templates function as the protectors by preventing the atoms from aggregation and then retain the size-dependent fluorescence properties during the process of AuNCs forming. Also, different templates produce kinds of gold nanoclusters with varying absorption and emission, which depend on not only the number of gold atom wrapped by different templates, but also the charge transferring between the templates and the metal core through AuS bonds [15].

As well as their favorable fluorescence, AuNCs indeed exhibit a series of advantages including fantastic catalytic performance, high quantum-yield, biocompatibility and water solubility [16–18]. Accordingly, these properties promote AuNCs to be widely employed in many fields such as biosensing, biolabeling, catalysis and beyond [19–21]. Especially, AuNCs are frequently designed and developed as the sensors towards numerous sensing applications, while their fluorescence variations of AuNCs serve as the sensing signals. The fluorescence of AuNCs could be quenched through different ways, which have been reported individually. For instance, Liu’s group [22] achieved the sensitive and selective detection of S2− by using AuNCs@BSA based on the reaction between S2− and AuNCs@BSA through forming Au2S precipitation, leading to the degradation of AuNCs@BSA. In addition, Hou’s group [23] detected trypsin by another route. Specifically, ZnS quantum dots (QDs) were prepared while BSA served as the template, which could be quenched owing to the digestion of BSA by trypsin. Besides, the GO serving as the quencher of AuNCs@peptide-MUA accomplished to assay MMP-9 by Min’s group [24]. However, scarce studies have systematically explored different fluorescence-quenching routes through one kind of gold nanoclusters.

Casein, accounting for about 80% of milk protein, has been recognized as one kind of amphiphilic copolymer with both the hydrophilic and hydrophobic amino acid residues [25,26]. As been reported, various proteins and amino acids have been applied as the templates for preparing nanoclusters, and casein possesses multiple amino acids, which potentiate it as a promising candidate of the templates for gold nanoclusters synthesis. Usually, several compounds containing sulphur obviously show the interaction with Au atom of AuNCs [27]. As being a routine organosulfur compound, thiourea may interact with gold nanoclusters which has been generally used in the agricultural field and mainly functioned as the pesticide [28,29]. Again, bromelain crudely extracted from the stems and immature of pineapples exhibits the proteolytic activity, and it probably hydrolyze some template proteins of the AuNCs, thus leading their fluorescence variations [30,31]. Besides, graphene oxide (GO) is well known as one type of effective energy acceptor, and has frequently played the role as the signal quencher towards kinds of AuNCs, on the basis of which many assays were built up [32,33].

Herein, we have creatively established a facile method of synthesizing AuNCs@casein, while casein served as the template of encapsulating Au atoms. Specifically, the as-prepared AuNCs@casein obviously showed the bright-red fluorescence and acceptable stability in aqueous solution, thus providing their potential for biosensing and bioimaging. Encouraged by their promising towards sensing of the current AuNCs, we have investigated the mechanisms of the fluorescence variations for AuNCs@casein based on three different routes in detail. Firstly, thiourea showed the interaction with the Au (I) in the core of AuNCs@casein, thus affecting the charge transfer between the templates and core. Secondly, bromelain could hydrolyze the template of AuNCs@casein, which leads to the destroyed structure of the current nanoclusters. Thirdly, graphene oxide with NH2 was used as the energy acceptors to quench AuNCs@casein by establishing a resonance energy transfer system. Taken together, the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs@casein could be achieved through the three routes above, which have systematically elucidated the multiple routes of the fluorescence quenching. Simultaneously, we have developed a fluorimetric sensing strategy for monitoring the levels of thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2 (Scheme 1 ).

Casein, bromelain, albumin from bovine serum (BSA), papain, pepsin, trypsin, polyetherimide (PEI), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and graphene oxide (powder, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4), sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, glutathione (GSH), threonine, valine and histidine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, U.S.). All the ions including Co2+, Cr2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, K+,Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, glacial acetic acid (HAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water, 18.25 MΩ cm, produced using an Aquapro AWL-0502-Pultrapure water system (Chongqing, China) was employed for the whole process of the experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were finished on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with an excitation slit set at 10 nm band pass and emission at 10 nm band pass in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. Meanwhile, UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Again, the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired by using a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Portland, America) at 200 KV. The size distribution data was obtained via a Zetasizer Nano Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern, England). Furthermore, elemental and functional group analyses were identified by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The quantum yields were determined by using Absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). A Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) was applied to measure the pH values of the aqueous solutions, and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) was used for blending the solutions. The thermostatic water bath (DF-101s) was bought from Gongyi Instrument Co., Ltd (Gongyi, China). The powder of AuNCs was obtained by lyophilization with PiloFD8-4.3V (Charlotte, USA).

All the glassware and magnetic stir bars for the experiments were thoroughly cleaned by freshly prepared aqua regia, and rinsed by the distilled water, and further subjected to oven-dried. Basically, 0.15 g casein was initially dissolved into 2 mL of Milli-Q purified water, and 1 mL NaOH solution (3 M) was added into the previous solution with vigorous stirring at 45 °C for 15 min, Then, a 0.3 mL of HAuCl4 solution (100 mM) was introduced, and this mixture was subjected to the incubation accompanied with vigorous stirring in a water bath at 45 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and followed by the supernatant filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the larger product. Finally, the purified AuNCs was collected and stored at 4 °C before further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between AuNCs and thiourea, bromelain, GO-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: During the procedure of investigating the fluorescence quenching for AuNCs@casein, thiourea, bromelain and graphene oxide with −NH2 as the representatives were selected, and the interaction between AuNCs@casein and thiourea was initially explored. To be specific, 800 μL of Milli-Q purified water and 100 μL of AuNCs were successively pipetted into each 1.5 mL of vial, and a series of 100 μL thiourea solutions with different concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 mM/L) were separately added into the previous vials. Then, the mixtures were incubated for 20 min in the 30 °C water-bath, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra of AuCs@casein were recorded, respectively. Moreover, the possible interference from K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, threonine, valine and histidine (10−4 M/L) were explored in the presence of AuNCs@casein.

Similarly, bromelain was subsequently introduced into AuNCs@casein. Firstly, 800 μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (50 mM, pH = 6) and 100 μL of AuNCs@casein were successively and parallelly pipetted into nine vials of 1.5-mL, and 100 μL of bromelain with different concentrations (70, 100, 300, 400, 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 5000 U/mL) was separately added into the nine vials. Then, these mixtures were incubated at 45 °C for 60 min and subjected to the fluorescence measurements. Finally, the possible interference originated from common metal ions and other substances (K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, threonine, valine and histidine with the concentration of 10−4 M) were investigated during the process of sensing bromelain.

Next, the solution of AuNCs@casein was added with GO-NH2. Initially, GO was equipped with amino on the surface, and its related modification was achieved according to a previous work [34]. To be specific, 8 mL of GO (1 mg/mL), 8 mL of PEI (10 mg/mL) and 4 mL of EDC (10 mg/mL) were sufficiently mixed at 25 °C for 30 min. Then, 12 mL of EDC (10 mg/mL) was added into the solution above, and the reaction was incubated at 25 °C for 24 h. Finally, the purified GO-NH2 was obtained through centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 30 min, followed by washing for six times with ultrapure water until its pH reached nearly neutral. Again, the product resuspended in the ultrapure water along with ultrasonic and stored at 4 °C for the further applications, and the working concentration of GO-NH2 was identified as 1 mg/mL. Furthermore, different GO-NH2 solutions (1 mg/mL) with varying volumes (35, 40, 55, 70, 100, 150, 200 and 300 μL) were separately added into the 1.5 mL centrifuge tube containing 100 μL AuNCs@casein, and then diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. Ultimately, the obtained-solutions kept vigorously shaking for 20 min and subjected to the fluorescence measurement. Furthermore, various substances (K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, threonine, valine and histidine with the concentration of 10−4 M) were separately introduced into the current sensing process to determine the possible effect.

Casein with multiple amino acid residues shows the functional groups of chelating metal, potentiating it as a favorable candidate of the template for preparing nanoclusters. Basically, AuNCs@casein was innovatively synthesized here based on a facile approach. Initially, the current AuNCs were subjected to the characterization by fluorescence spectroscopy. Specifically, the maximum emission appeared at 600 nm originated from an excitation wavelength of 335 nm (Fig. 1 A), whereas HAuCl4 or casein exhibited no fluorescence in the range of 550 nm–650 nm (Fig. S1, ESI†). Meanwhile, the aqueous solution of AuNCs@casein obviously emitted the red fluorescence (Fig. 1A, photograph a) with UV light (365 nm), while emerged as transparently yellow under daylight (Fig. 1A, photograph b). Simultaneously, a peak at 280 nm in the absorption spectrum of AuNCs@casein has been recorded (Fig. 1B), and there was apparently no surface plasmon resonance absorption peak at 520 nm, indicating the formation of Au nanoclusters rather than Au nanoparticles. In this case, casein served as not only the protective template but also the reduction reagent, as well as no reducing reagent introduced. To visualize AuNCs@casein prepared here, HR-TEM was subsequently employed, and the obtained images revealed that the majority of AuNCs@casein were well-dispersed and their average diameter were about 4.2 nm, it’s lattice spacing was 0.24 nm ascribing to the Au(111) (Fig. 1C). Likewise, their sizes were further confirmed as 3–8 nm with PDI = 0.53 (Fig. S2, ESI†) by dynamic light scattering (DLS), showing an acceptable agreement with that of HR-TEM. Besides, the quantum yield of AuNCs@casein was determined as 2.1% by Quantaurus-QY.

Next, the functional groups of the as-synthesized AuNCs were investigated by FTIR. To be specific, the peak at 3425 cm−1 (Fig. 1D) was ascribed to the characteristic absorption of OH stretching vibration, while the CO stretching vibration peaks located at 1654 cm−1. Similarly, the peaks at 1436 cm−1 were attributed to CH bending vibration. All the above analyses revealed the existence of carboxylic groups and hydroxyl groups for AuNCs@casein. Furthermore, XPS survey spectra were acquired towards elucidating the components of the current AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 1E, there emerged four peaks at 84.4, 284.8, 399.9, and 531.4 eV (attributed to Au 4f, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s respectively), demonstrating that AuNCs@casein was mainly composed of C, O, N and Au. Meanwhile, there were three peaks displayed at 284.7 eV, 285.4 eV and 289.1 eV in the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3A, ESI†), describing the existence of CC, CN/CO, and OCO on the surfaces of AuNCs@casein. Also, the N 1 s spectrum showed three peaks at 399.3 eV, 399.9 eV, and 400.5 eV, which were attributed to pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively [35] (Fig. S3B, ESI†). Again, the two peaks referred to 531.4 eV (CO) and 532.8 eV (CO) in the O1 s spectrum (Fig. S3C, ESI†). Taken the FTIR and XPS spectra together, these date demonstrated that AuNCs@casein were equipped with the characteristic functional groups mainly including OH, COOH and NH2, which were originated from the protecting template of casein. In addition, the peaks at 84.0 eV and 85.1 eV as displayed in the Au 4f XPS could be assigned to the binding energies of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f7/2 by Au (0) and Au (I), suggesting the coexistence of Au (0) and Au (I) of AuNCs@casein (Fig. 1F) [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the synthesis conditions of AuNCs@casein
ce:section.ce:para: Next, a series of experiments were designed and performed to explore the optimal conditions towards synthesizing AuNCs. In detail, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNCs@casein exhibited change along with varying incubation temperatures, concentrations of NaOH, amounts of casein and incubation time (Fig. S4A–D, ESI†), indicating that the preparation of AuNCs@casein depended on the previously selected conditions. Ultimately, 45 °C, 0.6 M of NaOH, 0.15 g of casein and 5 h served as the optimized conditions for the AuNCs@casein synthesis.

To evaluate the capability of AuNCs@casein for bearing the circumstances, the further experiments were performed. In general, the current AuNCs exhibited being stable in the neutral or alkaline environment (Fig. S5A, ESI†), while it showed the slight alteration accompanied by different concentrations of NaCl (0.1–1 M) (Fig. S5B, ESI†). Moreover, the fluorescent intensities of AuNCs@casein showed scarce variation in the presence of different metal ions (Co2+, Cr2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, K+, Cu2+ and Ca2+, 10−4 M for each), and the similar phenomenon was observed while AuNCs@casein was incubated with varying temperatures (35 °C, 45 °C, 55 °C, 65 °C, 75 °C) (Fig. S5C–D, ESI†). Overall, the detailed exploration above suggested their satisfactory stability of AuNCs@casein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of AuNCs@casein through three routes
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the interaction process between AuNCs@casein and thiourea more clearly, a scheme has been raised at the very beginning (Fig. 2 A). Particularly, the fluorescence intensity at 600 nm decreased by 87% upon introducing thiourea with the concentration of 1 mM (Fig. 2B), demonstrating that thiourea could quench the fluorescence of AuNCs@casein. It was speculated that the sulfide of thiourea induced the fluorescence quenching of the current AuNCs owing to forming the Au-S bonds. To address whether the proposed explanation was correct or not, seven other related compounds including thioacetamide, Na2S, vitamin B5, procaine, nicotinic acid, Ca2+ and Ba2+ were further introduced. Thioacetamide and Na2S, both containing sulfur, indeed showed the phenomenon of quenching the fluorescence as thiourea, while vitamin B5, procaine, nicotinic acid, Ca2+ and Ba2+ scarcely affected the fluorescent signals of AuNCs@casein, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching here were probably due to the formation of Au-S (Fig. 2C). To achieve a further understanding, the XPS spectra of AuNCs@casein with or without thiourea were obtained (Fig. 2D), and the peak of Au 4f7/2 at 84.2 eV for AuNCs@casein was deconvoluted into two species of Au (0) and Au (I), as well as their area ratio determined as 1.427:1. In contrast, the area ratio of Au (0) and Au (I) rose to 2.235:1 with thiourea added, and the Au (0) species consequently increased from 58.8% to 69.1%, demonstrating that thiourea has interacted with Au (I) of AuNCs@casein, thus forming Au-S [37].

Considering the template of AuNCs@casein is one kind of protein, we also addressed whether AuNCs@casein could be quenched through breaking their templates or not. Being well known, bromelain exhibits the proteolytic activity that allows it for hydrolyzing proteins, thus this enzyme has been initially introduced here (Fig. 3 A). Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@casein decreased obviously (Fig. 3B) as well as the addition of bromelain (500 U/mL), suggesting that their templates could be destroyed by bromelain (Fig. 3C). To further verify this possible principle, four other proteins including bovine serum albumin (BSA), trypsin, papain and pepsin have been employed and subjected to the same process. Again, there emerged a certain decrease for the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@casein by trypsin, papain and pepsin due to these proteins played the roles as the proteases. However, BSA showed scarce effect on the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@casein since it is just one routine protein without the proteolytic activity. Furthermore, their average size of AuNCs@casein with bromelain dramatically varied to 38.9 ± 1.7 nm, while the size of AuNCs@casein appeared as 4.2 ± 1.4 nm, describing the obvious aggregations of AuNCs@casein (Fig. 3D). Taken together, these results suggested that the templates of AuNCs@casein could be destroyed by the proteases especially bromelain, which induced the aggregations of Au atoms occurring, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs@casein [38].

To further broaden the routes of the fluorescence quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system has been subsequently established. Since GO-NH2 generally serves as an effective energy acceptor, AuNCs@casein exhibited the fluorescent signal, which has been recognized as the energy donors. Hence, GO-NH2 was introduced here for identifying its response to AuNCs@casein (Fig. 4 A). Initially, GO-NH2 was prepared by modifying GO with the amino groups on their surfaces. In particular, the zeta potential of GO varied from −30.6 mV to 27.8 mV through its reaction with PEI, demonstrating that GO was successfully equipped with the NH2 groups (Fig. S6A, ESI†). As a result, GO exhibited the negative charge and could not directly interact with AuNCs@casein of the negative charge (-39.3 mV), thus the distance between AuNCs and GO could not meet the requirement of the energy transferring (Fig. S6B, ESI†). Therefore, there appeared little variation for the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs. In contrast, the positive charge of GO-NH2 showed the interaction with AuNCs@casein, leading to the energy transferring from AuNCs@casein to GO-NH2 occurring and the quenched fluorescence of AuNCs (Fig. 4B) [24,39–41]. Thus, this result suggested that introducing the energy acceptor into AuNCs provided another way for affecting their fluorescence signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the sensing system for thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the previous phenomena, we addressed that whether AuNCs@casein could be employed for sensing thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2 or not. Firstly, the analytical conditions including the incubation time, temperature and pH between AuNCs and thiourea have been investigated towards obtaining the optimal sensing performance. Consequently, the variation of the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum while the reaction time of 35 min, incubation temperature of 45 °C, pH of 8 served as the optimal conditions (Fig. S7A–C, ESI†). According to these preferential conditions, the assaying model for thiourea has been successfully established. Specifically, F0-F decrease versus the concentrations displayed a linear range of 10 μM–1 mM with its correlation coefficient of 0.969 (Fig. 5 A and B), and the detection limit was 9.7 μΜ at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Meanwhile, the related images for the linear analyses were recorded under daylight and UV-lamp, indicating an agreement with the fluorescent change (Fig. 5C). Also, the possible effect originated from coexisting substance on measuring thiourea was tested, and the corresponding experiments were designed while thiourea (1 mM) was in the presence of K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, threonine, valine and histidine (10−4 M), respectively. As a result, there existed no obvious variation of the fluorescence signals, indicating that coexisting substance showed little effect on the detections of thiourea (Fig. S8, ESI†). Thus, AuNCs@casein may serve as a probe for sensing thiourea towards other purpose.

Similarly, we further employed AuNCs@casein for sensing bromelain. To obtain the optimized detecting conditions, the effect of the incubation time and temperature on the detections were investigated. Accordingly, 5 h and 60 °C served as the optimal conditions for the following experiments (Fig. S9A–B, ESI†). Furthermore, the standard curve of this sensing model was built up by introducing various concentrations of bromelain (7 U/mL, 10 U/mL, 30 U/mL, 40 U/mL, 50 U/mL, 100 U/mL, 200 U/mL, 300 U/mL and 500 U/mL). The F0-F of AuNCs@casein regularly decreased with the concentrations of bromelain varying from 7 U/mL to 500 U/mL, resulting in a typical dose-response curve together with its correlation coefficient of 0.982 (Fig. 5D and E), and the detection limit was 6.87 U/mL at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Simultaneously, the color of the AuNCs@casein solution changed from transparently yellow to yellow-brown accompanied with the increased bromelain under daylight (Fig. 5F, a), while the fluorescence of AuNCs@casein decreased under UV light (Fig. 5F, b). Again, the interferences experiments were further conducted for testing other possible interferences from K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, threonine, valine and histidine with the concentrations of 10−4 M, and the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@casein scarcely changed (Fig. S10, ESI†). Hence, these results described that the AuNCs prepared here provided the potential for assaying bromelain.

Considering the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@casein showed response to different concentrations of GO-NH2, we further investigated the detection conditions including the reaction temperature, pH and incubation time while the concentration of GO-NH2 was 200 μg/mL. By using the optimizing procedures, 10 min, 35 °C and pH 7.0 served as the optimized conditions for the following analyses (Fig. S11A–C, ESI†). Besides, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs@casein decreased (F0-F) along with the logarithmic plots of GO-NH2 concentrations (35, 40, 55, 70, 100, 150, 200, 300 μg/mL) with its correlation coefficient of 0.9873 (Fig. 5G and H), and the detection limit was 33.8 μg/mL at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Simultaneously, the related change of the fluorescence and color for AuNC@casein were recorded with both daylight and UV light (Fig. 5I a and b). The foreign coexisting substance (K+, Na+, sucrose, lactose, glucose, glutamic acid, threonine, valine and histidine, o-diaminobenzene, m-diaminobenzene and procaine) possibly imposing effect on the measurement of GO-NH2 were separately introduced in the presence of GO-NH2 (200 μg/mL). As described in Fig. S12, there was little variation for the fluorescence intensity, suggesting the processing of detecting GO-NH2 was not affected by the coexistence substances. Moreover, the proposed method were compared with other sensors as Table S1 (ESI†), describing that the current AuNCs exhibited the acceptable linear range.

On the whole, we have successfully proposed an innovative strategy to synthesize the water-soluble fluorescent AuNCs@casein as well as casein innovatively serving as the template. Meanwhile, this AuNCs@casein exhibited the advantages including the bright-red fluorescence and satisfactory stability, and the synthesis procedure was facile and environmentally-friendly. Significantly, the fluorescence of this prepared AuNCs could be effectively quenched through three different routes, and the mechanism of three different ways leading to the fluorescence change were explored in detail, which included forming the bonds of Au-S, breaking the templates of AuNCs@casein, and achieving FRET by thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2. Besides, three different sensing routes have been successfully employed for sensing thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2. Theoretically, the current identification of the three sensing routes not only allowed the analyses of thiourea, bromelain and GO-NH2, but also provided the promising strategies for determining other targets using other metal nanoclusters on the basis of the above sensing mechanism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2018AC004), Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing (cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003), the Opening Research Project from the State Key Laboratory of Silkworm Genome Biology (sklsgb161718-6).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.05.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Growth of highly fluorescent polyethylene glycol- and zwitterion-functionalized gold nanoclusters
